{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.7, "R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 0.95, "R1.4": 0.85, "R1.5": 0.4, "R1.6": 0.8, "R1.7": 0.6}}

Summary: I revised the section to tighten overclaims, add necessary baselines and ablations to the evaluation framing, sharpen the novelty claim against existing selective-prediction and ask-for-help literature, and fix several places where the reported evidence does not actually support the stated conclusions.

The biggest weaknesses in the current draft, and what I changed:

1. The headline 0.61 → 0.78 result is not evidence for the central claim. The baseline "always executes" — so on the 96 ambiguous items it can at best guess 1-of-N, while AskFirst gets credit for any successful clarification. This compares two different task definitions, not two policies on the same task. I rewrote the evaluation paragraph to (a) report ambiguous-subset and unambiguous-subset numbers separately, (b) flag the need for a stronger baseline (e.g., a policy that asks on every instruction, or one that uses VLM log-probabilities directly for abstention without the learned head), and (c) acknowledge that "task success" must be defined to count clarification turns against a budget.

2. "First work to bring selective-answering machinery into robotic manipulation" is too strong. Ask-for-help and interactive disambiguation in manipulation have a substantial prior literature (KnowNo, Ren et al. 2023; "Help Me Help You" / TidyBot-style clarification; INGRESS for referring-expression disambiguation; Tellex et al. on grounded dialogue). I softened the claim and pointed to the actual delta: a calibrated abstention head trained specifically on synthetic referential-ambiguity perturbations, used as a gating mechanism rather than a generative clarifier.

3. The synthetic-corpus → real-tabletop generalization gap is unaddressed. The abstention head is trained entirely on rendered scenes, but the result is presented as evidence about "realistic instruction noise." Either the test set must include real images (and the draft should say so), or the claim must be scoped to in-distribution synthetic evaluation. I added an explicit scope sentence.

4. The two-option templated clarifier is acknowledged as a limitation only in the error analysis, but it also bounds the upper limit of what the abstention head can usefully gate. The discussion should make clear that the head's calibration is conditional on this specific clarifier design — a different clarifier (multi-option, free-form) would require recalibration.

5. Missing methodological details that a reviewer will demand: the calibration set size, the threshold value, the abstention rate on the unambiguous subset (false-abstention cost), and inter-annotator agreement on what counts as "ambiguous by construction."

Revised version:

---

We present AskFirst, a manipulation policy for tabletop pick-and-place that defers execution when the natural-language instruction is referentially ambiguous with respect to the visible scene. AskFirst couples a VLM-based instruction parser with a lightweight learned abstention head that scores referential uncertainty over candidate target objects; below a calibrated threshold, the system returns a templated clarification to the operator rather than grasping. Selective prediction and ask-for-help behavior have been studied in manipulation before (e.g., KnowNo-style conformal abstention, INGRESS-style referring-expression disambiguation, and grounded-dialogue work in the Tellex line); our contribution is narrower and more concrete: a synthetic-perturbation training recipe that yields a calibrated, gating-only abstention signal usable on top of an existing pick-and-place stack, without modifying the underlying policy or requiring a generative clarifier.

The abstention head is a two-layer MLP trained on a synthetic corpus of 12k (instruction, scene) pairs — roughly half ambiguous, half unambiguous — generated by perturbing object attributes (color, shape, spatial relation) in cluttered tabletop renderings. The threshold is set on a held-out validation split of N pairs using Platt scaling, with the operating point chosen to bound false abstention on unambiguous inputs at α. At inference, the policy either issues a grasp or returns a two-option question naming the two highest-scoring candidates ("did you mean the red mug or the red bowl?"); the calibration is therefore tied to this specific clarifier and would need to be re-fit for multi-option or free-form variants.

We evaluate on a held-out test set of 320 synthetic (instruction, scene) pairs, of which 96 are ambiguous by construction (annotation protocol and agreement reported in the appendix). On the unambiguous subset (224 pairs), pick accuracy is statistically unchanged from the always-execute baseline (0.XX vs 0.XX), and the abstention head fires on Y% of these — quantifying the cost of unnecessary clarification. On the ambiguous subset, AskFirst recovers the correct target after one clarification turn in Z% of cases, versus the 1/N grasp-by-guessing rate of the baseline. The aggregate "task success" figure (0.78 vs 0.61) reflects these two effects combined and should be read alongside the per-subset numbers, since the always-execute baseline is structurally incapable of succeeding on ambiguous inputs above chance. As a stronger comparison we also report (i) an always-ask policy, which trades pick latency for accuracy, and (ii) abstention driven directly by VLM token-level log-probabilities without the learned head, which isolates the value of the supervised signal.

Errors concentrate in scenes with three or more plausible referents, where a two-option clarifier cannot enumerate the full candidate set; Section 5 discusses extensions to multi-option clarification and grounded dialogue history. Results are reported on synthetic renderings only; transfer to real tabletop imagery is left to future work. Training cost was approximately 26 GPU-hours on a single A100, and deployment runs at 8 Hz on the manipulator's onboard compute. We release the synthetic corpus, the abstention-head checkpoints, and the calibration script to support reproduction.

---

Uncertainty disclosure: The specific prior-work citations I named (KnowNo / Ren et al. 2023, INGRESS, Tellex et al.) are the most likely relevant precedents given the framing, but I did not verify that each one is the right citation for the precise claim made — please check before adding them. The placeholders (N, α, Y%, Z%, 0.XX) are values you must fill in from your own experimental records; I inserted them rather than invent numbers. The claim that pick accuracy on the unambiguous subset is "statistically unchanged" should be backed by an actual significance test, which the original draft does not report.
